transcript.transcript: This is oral history. I am Denise Smith. Today is April 23rd, 1973. I am going to interview Mr. Howie Kent. The general topic will be Bonners Ferry, Idaho in the early 1900s. Mr. Kemp, when were you born? I was born in Bonners Ferry in 1897. And how long did you live in Bonners? Practically all my life. Where were your parents born? My father was born in Wisconsin. My mother was born in the Dow Gorge and when did your parents come to Barclay? 1891. Yeah, yeah, mr. Can't we do please tell me a little bit about your earliest recollection of the business establishment of our say Yes the bar place 1891 Yes, as I remember the local business WL Camino Camino owner of a large I remember them on the local business. This was back as far as I can recall. W.L. Canina, owner of a large and prosperous general merchandise store, which had handled everything from patent medicine to grain and hay. I well remember the ginger snack cooking in a large barrel. He told me cooking was his thing. I said, I'm all right. I sold his jellies and small wooden buckets and cans. I sold his candies and huge, innocent flasks. And he told me the pound. Once the gun dropped, the other demons didn't speak. So the can is huge in its size and so is the pound. Once again, he dropped the other beans in six separate mints. Crunch off was served to be had by some military who were reluctant. Coffee came in bulk in one-pound packages. Both had to be ground at the store as always in a roasted bean form. And J.F. Cook's corn pan at a small goods store and Mr. Cook's offices located. Mr. Cook had to have both of his friends postmasters. I might add that Mr. Cook was a college graduate from pharmacy. And was Mr. Cook, was he the first new doctor in the United States? He was. I see. He was the only one And then Joe Joe a hardwood Both captain on the Cousin River He built a general merchandise store and handled just about the same people. Mr. Peer did. And another merchant was Mr. Van Gaskin and the other store And about the same supplies Tom Holland you had a The coating store the channel was everything And that was a good guy kept upon his credit W.F. Ellerton had a blacksmith shop and a shivery place down Main Street. He had a buzzer and workhorses and that type. And this is Ellerton's wife, Franny. She baked bread and she sold bread, homemade bread, cookies and donuts. Those were very huge, they were 10 cents a donut to us. 10 cents a dozen, pie at 15, that was great. Did you camp with any hotels in the North Area at this time? Reviews And Oh, yes They could be operated on the better hotels and dining rooms known as the International At that time I am returning there was no electricity or pipe water All hotels operated with a six-room bowl and kitchen lamps in each room Eating was a problem, especially in the winter. Every block of the other side was so heated that it circulated so slowly through the upper rooms. This hotel burned to the ground a few years later. Another hotel that still stands on Main Street was the Casey Hotel, built and operated by Jim Casey. At that time of its construction, It was considered one of the best hotels that had a Spokane Had a large dining room also Sloan bigger party Leave the after-dates for those times and Then across the streets in there Was a Rather shoddy hotels known as the Bonner Hotel. This is Run by a Norwegian woman Very rough No one's up for the lumberjack. I'm out and then also I was thrown in and down father was a international hotel Operated by mr. Beaver No, they'll also front later so it's pretty well supplied with hotels yes there's one barbershop operated by the two Cain brothers Dell and Emory And how much did they charge for a haircut at that time? A haircut was 25 cents, shaved 15. What do you think it is now? Were there any other Jewish establishments that you can particularly remember? Oh yes, Charles of Callahan was a local real estate dealer and U.S. Commissioner. A.J. Kent, I think, was a real estate dealer. And in short, this was in 1905. This was your father at the time? Yes, this was my father. Matter of fact, he remained in that business until his death in 1940. Then I said I'm from there And Of course then on Main Street, there was a large building by the boy and the KP which made a substantial Improvement in town because they all contain both contain dance floors With the people in the garden What about Not if you said delivery wagon You can go and put it in your other list in the circle and pick up orders and deliver in what I say or whether perhaps I've used it in. Well, the hell, I'll tell you this, in 1904, there were about seven prunes. But that was just another year, and Bob was there going to deliver them. But 1905 and 6, the town took on a boom town atmosphere Too big a dent was put in place The Bonner Sherry lumber company a warehouse of concern from Wisconsin was laying the groundwork for a large sawmill, planer mill, and drive-shaft on the very edge of town. Also, they were already building logging camps for the preparation of the log supplies of the mill. In addition to this huge operation of Oakland International Railroad was building food on the lower end of town I'm sorry being a half of both projects memory and demand railroad building and lumberjacks along with mail workers Bonasera boosted seven slumes in 1907 for at the end of 1905. They had 17 stone Like all small towns I'm sure I had one main street with a big mouth But it's on each side in the year 1946 we had 34 big MacArthur's on Main Street Which consisted of 17 soon? The balance storage All buildings of same construction the board of sidewalks and dirt streets and by 1906 the Things were in full swing hundreds of railroad workers in lumberjacks in town on Saturday and Sunday Which is really something. We found people stayed away from Main Street at this time We can Days are badly not the workers on coming localized lies the Southern venture downtown Fortunately grocery stores sent a cook each morning to Northland, their home, to pick up the orders. Even for the day, each order was in, sold, and delivered later by horse and dozen. After the railroad was finished many Sloan Went out of business and the town set it back to a normal Condition 1915 local option came in and put all the kids out of business. But I might add that during these saloon days, I've had three policemen. And that was hardly enough. And We had a small jail Over by the river bank and they put them in there with you had to put a guard on them or break their way out Fights are common and a few killings and robberies going on all the time. Rolling drugs. It was really exciting. I see. You and me both know today little boys love to collect beer bottles and things like that. Did you do things like that when you were a child too? I like to say I did. That's where I made my money. There was a big coat brown beer bottle that was worth five cents a piece. And of course, glass hadn't been perfected and they wanted them returned. I was still playing whiskey glass, worth five cents. Make money because of all the films so I'm gathered up here by the sticks and everything get a can of coffee for him You mentioned something a little while ago about a boat capping was boating a method of transportation in those days? oh yes boating on the Kootenai River in the late 80s and early 90s was carried out rather extensively due to the fact that the vicinity of Nelson and casual British Columbia number of pollution mine mainly silver and red but unfortunately, there was a No other way to get the ore concentrate to the coma now But to by necessary hence by good normal a road to the coast and they only way to Find a poor body of the truth The To the only operate during a high water stage Giving the fact these bodies and boats require deep water the good known Will it spurred on Lying down to the boat liners to the or it could be transferred to cars In addition a number of large passengers come by the passenger car boat. The car boat most put into operation These boats were equipped with dining rooms and sleeping quarters in addition to large cargo seats. Please bear in mind There were no widening roads to Kootenai Lake, Canada from Bonner's Ferry at this time. The Nelson country was getting all its supplies by tote loadings from Spokane through the Cattle Bowl washing way. The Bolton-Barnes service to Bonner's Ferry really simplified the Nelson-related needs for transportation as a concern. Also, it created business for Bonner's Ferry. Not only all travel from Canada to Spokane was through here. A tremendous amount of merchandise was purchased through Canada. The oil was a good deal. So that's oil and hay, barrels of kerosene, mining machinery, and so on. Much being shipped up in the Southland West, the miners very much in the hotels in the West, increase their share of the transom business. Also, it creates a job here. I have here that some of the passenger boats are large enough to accommodate 100 passengers, if necessary. And I also sleep half of them. The dining room was very elaborate and served excellent meals. The distance between Nelson, BC by water was almost 200 miles. 100 miles by Kootenai River to Kootenai Lake. Then another 100 miles by Lake Juneau. A much beautiful trip to the show Holding all things are men and the great garden There was very low down this cooking Valley to the head of the Kootenai Lake. Happened February 11th, 1912. Did you like building that railroad? Yes, I enjoyed it. The reason, really the reason this railroad was built, Kootenai River would get low in the fall and freeze up? And there's no poster the mind of that go down a stop pile a hole So the great known hit on the railroad hit on the idea of building a line down through They did This Down But they did This permitted the year-round Transportation. Did they have that did they provide passenger service? Oh, no, it's not your service if They say the baggage car a combination baggage car and They're going on that year on business And also eliminated out of this boat transportation. I guess all that comes from a business To the other When the work about the start on this Project is Chinese coolies began drifting in the town at first not much attention was paid to them The numbers increased as such and it was quite apparent that the Chinese were planning to build that railroad. Well, the businessmen became alarmed. There was a nuisance around our main street. But along with them there was lots of all what we call nowadays Bohun and Greeks and Pollock's and they were really the spare spare railroad builders, But the project hadn't quite started but it's ready to take off anytime So as I say too much attention went paid to the Chinese at first within a few days, they kept flocking in and flocking in the business men became armed And not only that there was very much trouble between these other railroads like the Chinese cruise Beat them up and they were more quickly said they've gone strike to move in Yes, well, so that he meant all of you and the contractor for this job for the Great Northern, he had an office in Barnard's Prairie, so they sent a man over to have him come over to this meeting. And they told him, they were down the line, they said, we've got to do something about these Chinese. Well, he said that he had no knowledge of ordering Chinese labor, because labor was needed. And he could use them, but he didn't order them. He said, I'll exact. He said, they're taking care of them. These other workers, the polacks and the gander-mansions. So, he said, I'll, they're telling me how to get them out of town. He said, well, I'll do something about it in a day or two. They said no, not a day or two right now. And we said, well, there's no train through today. And they said, oh yes there is, there's a freight. And we said, they'll give them all the way to go over to the Great Northern, and they said, patiently, they can make preparations to pay their transportation out. They said, we'll get them out. And so we could see that he wanted to think that the businessmen and the bongos served in the workers' club. He said, all right, we'll do that, but how are you going to get them off you leave that to us we'll do that. So they took the guns and clubs and they went around town and they rounded up every Chinese in town and they were all dressed in black hats and blouses and baggy pants. Most of them carried a sack over their shoulder and stuff in them. They rounded them all up and you never heard such a chat on the internet. And you can open the carry cycle this Is on them all up universe that we've had in the United States of America, but then They took my old the beach people in the Box cars on the back tracks where they opened the door and loaded them in and then they'd get in and the fake wasn't due for an hour or two. So they took guard over them. When the fake came in by, they talked to the conduction and said, yes, you're hooked on to it. And they said, well, I took them. Nobody cared, but they took them. They're my attackers. So they did hook on. That was the last of the Chinese. We had orders not to come back. But that's the last the Chinese were puttled. Oh, two years later, there was a few kids, men and women, that went laundry and laundry and rest and they were two very good Chinese. And also, two or three young Hungarians that were used to puttled green stuff around by a ferry. There was a bamboo pole over the shore and a basket of beetroot and onions and everything. And the fun was very nice and clean. So they lived there many, many years. But today we don't have a tournament. Wasn't there a man by the name of Jumbo? Jumbo was a Chinese. He was six foot three, the biggest Chinaman I ever heard of. And he was a great fellow, Jumbo. Everybody liked him. He had a record. And the Indians didn't recognize him very much. He had a back room with a long table and he was a big man. Flies were very bad in those days and even the jumbos you had to watch the gravy. Full of flies. You mentioned the Indians. Would you like to talk a little bit about them now? Yes, they lived a long time, about three miles on what they called Mission Hill, due to the Indians. And there was approximately about 150 at that time, women, children and all. And there was only one small log cabin and the rest of the whole team And of course they lived out the country more last minute Great to come in town Well Get around town watching people It was great to come into town and go get around on the steps around town watching people. Some of the women folks around town would have the squads do their watching for him everything was a water source, okay? But you had to watch them very closely or they'd take the pack loose out of a Carry all and put sugar in and then put the fat goose back in. And that'd carry water. Just turn down the water. But they got the habit, after you hired them once, the squads would come back and sit on the porch and they know it wasn't moisture but they sit there and Talk to him and know that it was Stay there. You said that bring them on some beef they've eaten and they leave So that's going to be a lot of monotonous and And the Bucky Indians they carried water to the hotels But they drank quite a bit, that was the big problem with the Indians, was they were drinking. And of course there was always somebody to buy booze for them. And they'd get in fights and knife one another and kill one another. Or fall in the river, tip the canoe over the ground, it just got common. But now to the present time there isn't over 50 Indians left in Bonners Ferry now, the young bucks that are over there have died. And I think that's covered by the Indians. How about entertainment in those days, Mr. Kent? Did the Indians play any card in there? Yes, they did. Well, the entertainment in the early days was pretty young, with ice skating and sleigh rides in winter. Also, local talent shows were popular during the winter season. School dances were unheard of at that time. Birthdays were always a big event, but I was under 18, and the birthday party wasn't much. We're on her that at that time those days were always a busy fan. So that was under History party was a much also the young Bible classes were active Some of them were rather busy everyone in the records. Some of them were the rather busy times of the younger people, putting in the garden, and hay, and getting water, and there was always a much. But with all the killers, they found times of swimming, baseball, sitting in a room. Also, they would turn the empty church to sitting in a quick game. Or somewhere close by, always a pop-up affair. Now, the older generation lacked very little. They had to admit to church affairs, lodges, activities, fasted socials, mass grades, square dances, and... And of course, if there is larger activity, that's the social masquerade square dance is in. So I am, and they anticipated it, and I'm one of the ones skating and sky riding, and coasting downhill on a homemade box sled, which is enjoyed by both young and old. There ain't nothing but teens in that country, and I know teens are out there, and they expect all the hills and floodgates and roads down there. Dangerous running into a teen. No teams are out. We had access to all the hills and slide down the road without any danger of running into a team. Also, traveling folks, shows such as men's groups, it's good excitement, hypnotic, always created excitement, plays like the girls the Golden West and so on all shows at that time kids own band sometimes the band just play for a dance after the show Outside that and the kingdom is always enjoyed plus a July was was and show outside that entertainment was always enjoyed. Fourth of July was an unforgettable occasion. We started off with a local digging up there and making a very big reality show. I'd go to a hall balcony on Main Street. Following this would be some places that are already known. They all held on Main Street. Back places, potato races, and basketball matches. And the basketball matches were always on a raised platform. Usually an Indian against a white, which was exciting. The streets were decorated one end of the other with buttons and flags and so on. The prison really missed mainstream in those days. In the afternoon, below town was the racetrack. All around the racetrack, Indian people could take a stand. They'd move in and a body from there, and listen for their defeat. They'd mingle with all kinds of participating all events and they did they got this morning's event and fundraise here five money and all the churches one of the big events for the Indian first bus canoe race across the clean river and back here they've been about 1, 000 feet wide at this point Priced at $25 for first $15 a second $10 or 30 really incurred damage especially in June That same Canadian race was really something to watch everyone's trying for his favorite Indian We're gonna do a late night evening. The end of the Padana parlor. We dance until dark. By night, I have a hot dog and hamburger, stand, and boots ready for operation and various locations around town. Nobody has a go on home-baked cocktail Popcorn, Candy, and Soda why we might be much for the youngsters. I was quite good. But, yeah, sometimes these celebrations last two days. I often wonder what has happened to the 4th of July They did made up of Valley merchants and farmers and knowledge at the uniform Overall predominated and and the shoes have been used very barefoot Well, they were merchants and farmers and all. But the uniformed, or something, was the overall predominated. And the shoes, some of the uniforms, were barefooted. But it was a lot of fun. And the film keepers always seemed like they were a couple tagged the beer on tap, however, for the players. And there was a big tin cup. Along came the last part of the game. Some of the participants were about to stagger off to their positions, but everybody had cotton. Since the town of Monash area is so close to the St. Louis Mississippi, I'm sure you must remember several of the floods that have career. Would you tell me about both please?

story.title: Kent_Howard_OH 1663 Side A
story.description: Recorded on April 23, 1973. Topic is Bonner's Ferry.
